questions.question: If you dilute 750 ml of a 10% v/v solution to 1000 ml, what will be the percentage strength of the new volume?

steps.text: Recognize that the problem involves dilution.
steps.content: Look for key terms such as "diluted," "stock preparation," "final concentration," or "prepare from a stronger preparation." Dilution problems typically involve a higher concentration (stock) and a lower concentration (final product). For this question, you can tell this is a dilution problem by looking at the following key terms:

steps.text: Understand the goal of the problem and determine what you are being asked to find. Identify the Q1, C1, Q2, and C2, and convert units as needed for the dilution formula Q1 × C1 = Q2 × C2
steps.hint: Q1 = 750 ml, C1 = 10%, Q2 = 1000 ml, C2 is unknown. All units are in the correct format, so no conversion is needed.

steps.text: Use the dilution formula and solve for the unknown

questions.question: If 100 ml of a 1:25 w/v solution is diluted to 1000 ml, what is the ratio strength of the new solution (w/v)?

steps.text: Recognize that the problem involves dilution.
steps.content: Look for key terms such as "diluted," "stock preparation," "final concentration," or "prepare from a stronger preparation." Dilution problems typically involve a higher concentration (stock) and a lower concentration (final product). For this question, you can tell this is a dilution problem by looking at the following key terms:

steps.text: Understand the goal of the problem and determine what you are being asked to find. Identify the Q1, C1, Q2, and C2, and convert units as needed for the dilution formula Q1 × C1 = Q2 × C2
steps.hint: Q1 = 100 mL, C1 = 1:25, Q2 = 1000 mL, C2 is unknown. All units are in the correct format, so no conversion is needed.

steps.text: Use the dilution formula and solve for the unknown
steps.hint: 100 mL × 1/25 = 1000 mL × 1/X, solving for X: X = (1000 × 25)/100 = 250, therefore the ratio strength is 1:250

steps.hint: Q1 = 200 mL, C1 = 1:20, Q2 = 750 mL, C2 is unknown. All units are in the correct format, so no conversion is needed.

steps.text: Use the dilution formula and solve for the unknown
steps.hint: 200 mL × 1/20 = 750 mL × 1/X, solving for X: X = (750 × 20)/200 = 75, therefore the ratio strength is 1:75

questions.question: You diluted a 89 ml sample to 150 ml. The percentage strength of the diluted sample is 20% v/v. What was the strength of the original sample? Round final answer to the nearest tenth.

steps.text: Recognize that the problem involves dilution.
steps.content: Look for key terms such as "diluted," "stock preparation," "final concentration," or "prepare from a stronger preparation." Dilution problems typically involve a higher concentration (stock) and a lower concentration (final product). For this question, you can tell this is a dilution problem by looking at the following key terms:

steps.text: Understand the goal of the problem and determine what you are being asked to find. Identify the Q1, C1, Q2, and C2, and convert units as needed for the dilution formula Q1 × C1 = Q2 × C2
steps.hint: Q1 = 89 mL, C1 is unknown, Q2 = 150 mL, C2 = 20%. All units are in the correct format, so no conversion is needed.

steps.text: Use the dilution formula and solve for the unknown
steps.hint: 89 mL × C1 = 150 mL × 20%, C1 = (150 × 20)/89 = 33.7% (rounded to nearest tenth)

questions.question: One gallon of a 25% w/v solution was evaporated so that the solution will have a strength of 60%. What is the new volume in milliliters? Round final answer to the nearest whole number.

Concentration by evaporation problems typically involve a lower concentration (initial) and a higher concentration (final product). For this question, you can tell this is a concentration problem by looking at the following key terms:

steps.text: Convert all units to standard units (i.e., g and mL)
steps.hint: Convert 1 gallon to milliliters using the conversion factor: 1 gallon = 3785 mL

steps.text: Understand the goal of the problem and determine what you are being asked to find. Identify the Q1, C1, Q2, and C2, and convert units as needed for the dilution formula Q1 × C1 = Q2 × C2
steps.hint: Q1 = 3785 mL, C1 = 25%, Q2 is unknown, C2 = 60%. All units are now in the correct format.

questions.question: How much atropine is required to make 240 mL in such a way that when 1 teaspoon of the solution is diluted to 1 pint, it gives a 1:500 solution? Round final answer to the nearest hundredth.

steps.text: Recognize that the problem involves a two-step dilution.
steps.content: Look for key terms that indicate multiple dilution steps. For this question, you can tell this is a two-step dilution problem by looking at the following key terms:

steps.text: Convert all units to standard units (i.e., convert teaspoon to mL, pint to mL, and ratio to percentage)
steps.hint: Convert 1:500 to percentage by dividing 1 by 500 and multiplying by 100 = 0.2%

steps.text: Understand the goal of the problem and determine what you are being asked to find. Identify the Q1, C1, Q2, and C2, and convert units as needed for the dilution formula Q1 × C1 = Q2 × C2
steps.hint: Q1 = 5 mL (1 teaspoon), C1 is unknown, Q2 = 473 mL (1 pint), C2 = 0.2% (1:500). All units are in the correct format.

steps.text: Use the dilution formula and solve for the unknown
Set up the ratio and proportion
Solve for X
steps.hint: Divide 18.92 g/100 mL by 240 mL to find the answer in g. The answer is 45.41 g.

steps.text: Use the dilution formula and solve for the unknown
Set up the ratio and proportion
steps.hint: 20 g/100 mL = X g/75 mL
